cards.back: Frequencies ω₁, ω₂ where P = Pmax/2, equivalently I = Imax/√2 = 0.707·Imax.
B = ω₂ − ω₁ = R/L = ω₀/Q (rad/s). Independent of C.
Q = ω₀/B. Higher Q = narrower bandwidth = sharper selectivity.
ω₀ = √(ω₁·ω₂). The resonant frequency is exactly the geometric mean of the half-power frequencies.
B = R/L. Larger R → wider bandwidth → lower Q → less selective. Smaller R → narrower → higher Q.
|Z| = √2·R at ω₁ and ω₂. The reactance magnitude equals R at these points.
Gv(jω) = 1/[1 + jQ(ω/ω₀ − ω₀/ω)]. Unity gain at ω₀, −3dB at half-power frequencies.
φ = ±45° at ω₁ and ω₂. At resonance φ = 0°. Transitions from −45° through 0° to +45°.
ω₁ ≈ ω₀ − B/2, ω₂ ≈ ω₀ + B/2. Valid when Q >> 1 (response is nearly symmetric).
How well the circuit passes signals at ω₀ while rejecting other frequencies. High Q = high selectivity = narrow bandwidth.
